text:
The CPCB AQI condenses eight pollutants (PM2.5, PM10, NO2, SO2, CO, O3, NH3, Pb)
into a single 0–500 index across six categories. The reported AQI is the maximum
sub-index; the pollutant driving it is the "dominant pollutant".

metadata.title: CPCB National Air Quality Index — Categories and Health Advisory
metadata.section: CPCB National Air Quality Index (AQI) — Categories & Health Advisory

citation: CPCB National Air Quality Index — Categories and Health Advisory — CPCB — 2015-04-06
section: CPCB National Air Quality Index (AQI) — Categories & Health Advisory

text:
- **Good (0–50):** Minimal impact.
- **Satisfactory (51–100):** Minor breathing discomfort to sensitive people.
- **Moderate (101–200):** Breathing discomfort to people with lung disease
 (asthma), and discomfort to heart-disease patients, children and older adults.
- **Poor (201–300):** Breathing discomfort to most on prolonged exposure.
- **Very Poor (301–400):** Respiratory illness on prolonged exposure; more
 pronounced effects in people with lung and heart disease.
- **Severe (401–500):** Affects healthy people and seriously impacts those with
 existing diseases; may cause respiratory effects even with light activity.

metadata.title: CPCB National Air Quality Index — Categories and Health Advisory

citation: CPCB National Air Quality Index — Categories and Health Advisory — CPCB — 2015-04-06

text:
- At Poor and above, sensitive groups (children, elderly, pregnant women, and
 people with asthma, COPD or heart disease) should minimise outdoor exertion.
- At Very Poor / Severe, everyone should avoid outdoor physical activity, keep
 windows closed during peak hours, use N95/FFP2 masks outdoors, and run indoor
 air purifiers where available.
- PM2.5-dominated pollution penetrates deep into the lungs and bloodstream; it is
 the primary driver of health risk in Delhi's winter smog.

*Summary of CPCB's AQI scheme; refer to the official CPCB AQI documentation for
exact pollutant breakpoints.*

metadata.title: CPCB National Air Quality Index — Categories and Health Advisory

citation: CPCB National Air Quality Index — Categories and Health Advisory — CPCB — 2015-04-06

text:
The Delhi Pollution Control Committee (DPCC), ahead of each winter/Diwali season,
notifies a complete ban on the production, storage, sale and use of all types of
firecrackers (including so-called "green" crackers) in the NCT of Delhi.

metadata.title: DPCC Ban on Firecrackers in Delhi
metadata.section: DPCC — Ban on Manufacture, Sale and Use of Firecrackers

citation: DPCC Ban on Firecrackers in Delhi — DPCC — 2023-09-11
section: DPCC — Ban on Manufacture, Sale and Use of Firecrackers

text:
- The ban typically runs through the winter high-pollution season (covering
 Diwali) and is enforced by Delhi Police and DPCC teams.
- Online sale and delivery of firecrackers into Delhi is also prohibited.
- Firecracker bursting during Diwali causes a sharp overnight spike in PM2.5 and
 metallic aerosols, compounding the winter smog and stubble-transport load.
- Violations attract penalties under the Air (Prevention and Control of
 Pollution) Act and the Explosives Act.

metadata.title: DPCC Ban on Firecrackers in Delhi

citation: DPCC Ban on Firecrackers in Delhi — DPCC — 2023-09-11

text:
This is the primary regulatory lever for the Diwali firecracker spike, a
short-lived but intense combustion source visible in ward-level AQI around the
festival window.

*Summary of recurring DPCC firecracker notifications; consult the current-season
DPCC order for exact dates and scope.*

metadata.title: DPCC Ban on Firecrackers in Delhi

citation: DPCC Ban on Firecrackers in Delhi — DPCC — 2023-09-11

text:
The Graded Response Action Plan (GRAP) is the emergency air-quality response
framework for Delhi-NCR, invoked by the Commission for Air Quality Management
(CAQM). Stage I applies when the AQI is in the **Poor (201–300)** band.

metadata.title: GRAP Stage I — Poor Air Quality (AQI 201–300)

citation: GRAP Stage I — Poor Air Quality (AQI 201–300) — CAQM — 2022-08-05

text:
- Enforce dust-mitigation norms at all construction and demolition (C&D) sites;
 cover materials, use anti-smog guns on large sites.
- Mechanised / vacuum sweeping of roads and daily water sprinkling on identified
 dust-prone corridors.
- Strict control of dust at C&D sites larger than 500 m²; sites must be
 registered on the dust-monitoring portal.
- Ban on open burning of solid waste and biomass; enforce penalties.
- Ensure proper functioning of pollution-control devices in industries and
 thermal power plants.
- Tighten enforcement of PUC (Pollution Under Control) norms; identify and
 penalise visibly polluting vehicles.
- Restrict use of diesel generator (DG) sets except for essential/emergency use.

metadata.title: GRAP Stage I — Poor Air Quality (AQI 201–300)

citation: GRAP Stage I — Poor Air Quality (AQI 201–300) — CAQM — 2022-08-05

text:
Sensitive groups (children, elderly, people with respiratory or cardiac
conditions) should reduce prolonged or heavy outdoor exertion. General public is
largely unaffected but may notice minor discomfort.

*Summary of the revised GRAP schedule notified by CAQM; consult the official CAQM
order for the authoritative and current text.*

metadata.title: GRAP Stage I — Poor Air Quality (AQI 201–300)

citation: GRAP Stage I — Poor Air Quality (AQI 201–300) — CAQM — 2022-08-05

text:
Stage II measures are added on top of all Stage I measures when the AQI enters
the **Very Poor (301–400)** band.

metadata.title: GRAP Stage II — Very Poor Air Quality (AQI 301–400)

citation: GRAP Stage II — Very Poor Air Quality (AQI 301–400) — CAQM — 2022-08-05

text:
- Intensify mechanised sweeping and daily water sprinkling on roads, especially
 at hotspots and identified dust corridors.
- Increase parking fees to discourage private vehicle use, coupled with augmented
 CNG / electric bus and Metro services (additional fleet, higher frequency).
- Ban the use of coal and firewood, including in tandoors at hotels, restaurants
 and open eateries.
- Restrict / regulate use of diesel generator sets across sectors (with defined
 exemptions for essential services and emergencies).
- Deploy traffic police at identified congestion points to smooth flow and cut
 idling emissions.
- Target enforcement at the 13 identified pollution hotspots in Delhi.

metadata.title: GRAP Stage II — Very Poor Air Quality (AQI 301–400)

citation: GRAP Stage II — Very Poor Air Quality (AQI 301–400) — CAQM — 2022-08-05

text:
People with heart or lung disease, older adults and children should avoid
prolonged or heavy outdoor exertion; others should reduce it. Consider N95
masks outdoors and keep windows closed during peak pollution hours.

*Summary of the revised GRAP schedule notified by CAQM.*

metadata.title: GRAP Stage II — Very Poor Air Quality (AQI 301–400)

citation: GRAP Stage II — Very Poor Air Quality (AQI 301–400) — CAQM — 2022-08-05

text:
Stage III adds stringent restrictions on top of Stage I and II when the AQI
enters the **Severe (401–450)** band.

metadata.title: GRAP Stage III — Severe Air Quality (AQI 401–450)

citation: GRAP Stage III — Severe Air Quality (AQI 401–450) — CAQM — 2022-08-05

text:
- Ban on construction and demolition (C&D) activities in Delhi-NCR, except for
 essential projects (railways, metro, airports, national security, healthcare)
 and non-polluting activities such as plumbing, interior and electrical work.
- Ban on operation of stone crushers and all mining / associated activities.
- Restriction on **BS-III petrol and BS-IV diesel** light motor vehicles
 (4-wheelers) in Delhi and neighbouring districts; enforced by traffic police.
- Closure of brick kilns, hot-mix plants and industries not running on approved
 cleaner fuels (unless connected to gas supply).
- State governments may consider restricting classes up to Grade 5 to a hybrid
 (physical + online) mode.
- Intensify all Stage I–II dust, sweeping and public-transport measures.

metadata.title: GRAP Stage III — Severe Air Quality (AQI 401–450)

citation: GRAP Stage III — Severe Air Quality (AQI 401–450) — CAQM — 2022-08-05

text:
Everyone should avoid outdoor physical activity; sensitive groups should remain
indoors and keep activity low. Use air purifiers where available; wear N95 masks
if going outdoors is unavoidable.

*Summary of the revised GRAP schedule notified by CAQM.*

metadata.title: GRAP Stage III — Severe Air Quality (AQI 401–450)

citation: GRAP Stage III — Severe Air Quality (AQI 401–450) — CAQM — 2022-08-05

text:
Stage IV is the most stringent level, adding emergency restrictions on top of
Stages I–III when the AQI exceeds **450**.

metadata.title: GRAP Stage IV — Severe+ Air Quality (AQI > 450)

citation: GRAP Stage IV — Severe+ Air Quality (AQI > 450) — CAQM — 2022-08-05

text:
- Ban on entry of truck traffic into Delhi (except trucks carrying essential
 commodities / providing essential services, and all LNG/CNG/electric trucks).
- Ban on Delhi-registered diesel-run medium and heavy goods vehicles, except
 those carrying essential commodities.
- Ban on all construction and demolition activities, including essential public
 projects such as highways, flyovers, pipelines and power transmission.
- Restrictions extended to BS-III/BS-IV four-wheelers as in Stage III, with
 tighter enforcement.
- State governments to consider: closing schools/colleges, non-emergency
 commercial activity, and allowing **50% of staff to work from home**.
- Consider additional emergency measures: odd-even vehicle rationing, halting
 non-essential offices.

metadata.title: GRAP Stage IV — Severe+ Air Quality (AQI > 450)

citation: GRAP Stage IV — Severe+ Air Quality (AQI > 450) — CAQM — 2022-08-05

text:
Public-health emergency for air quality. Everyone should stay indoors as far as
possible, run purifiers, and strictly avoid outdoor exertion. Sensitive groups
are at serious risk.

*Summary of the revised GRAP schedule notified by CAQM.*

metadata.title: GRAP Stage IV — Severe+ Air Quality (AQI > 450)

citation: GRAP Stage IV — Severe+ Air Quality (AQI > 450) — CAQM — 2022-08-05

text:
NCAP is India's national framework (launched 2019 by the Ministry of Environment,
Forest and Climate Change) to tackle air pollution across cities in a
time-bound, target-oriented manner.

metadata.title: National Clean Air Programme (NCAP)

citation: National Clean Air Programme (NCAP) — MoEFCC — 2019-01-10

text:
- Covers 130+ non-attainment cities that consistently exceeded national ambient
 air-quality standards for PM10 / PM2.5.
- Original target: 20–30% reduction in PM2.5 and PM10 concentrations by 2024
 (against a 2017 baseline); subsequently revised toward a **40% reduction in
 particulate levels by 2025–26** for many cities.
- Each city prepares a **City Action Plan** covering road dust, vehicular
 emissions, industrial emissions, construction dust, biomass and waste burning.
- Emphasis on source-apportionment studies, expanded monitoring networks, and
 public disclosure of air-quality data.
- Funding tied to performance; central assistance to municipal bodies for
 mitigation (mechanised sweeping, greening, dust control).

metadata.title: National Clean Air Programme (NCAP)

citation: National Clean Air Programme (NCAP) — MoEFCC — 2019-01-10

text:
NCAP explicitly calls for source-specific, locally-tailored interventions — the
kind a ward-level forecasting + attribution system is designed to inform.

*Summary of NCAP; refer to the official MoEFCC / CPCB NCAP documents for details.*

metadata.title: National Clean Air Programme (NCAP)

citation: National Clean Air Programme (NCAP) — MoEFCC — 2019-01-10

text:
Open burning of paddy straw in Punjab, Haryana and western UP during the
October–November harvest is a dominant external contributor to Delhi's winter
PM2.5 when north-westerly winds transport smoke into the NCR.

metadata.title: Stubble (Paddy Residue) Burning Management in NCR
metadata.section: Stubble / Paddy-Residue Burning Management (NCR & adjoining areas)

citation: Stubble (Paddy Residue) Burning Management in NCR — CAQM — 2021-09-13
section: Stubble / Paddy-Residue Burning Management (NCR & adjoining areas)

text:
- Promote in-situ management with the **Pusa bio-decomposer** and crop-residue
 management (CRM) machinery (Happy Seeder, Super Seeder, balers).
- Ex-situ use of paddy straw as biomass fuel in power plants and industry, and in
 compressed biogas.
- Financial incentives / disincentives to farmers; monitoring of fire counts via
 satellite (VIIRS/MODIS) with district-level accountability.
- Red-entry / bonus schemes and targeted enforcement in high-fire districts.

metadata.title: Stubble (Paddy Residue) Burning Management in NCR
metadata.section: Measures directed by CAQM and state governments

citation: Stubble (Paddy Residue) Burning Management in NCR — CAQM — 2021-09-13
section: Measures directed by CAQM and state governments

text:
When upwind fire counts are high and winds are north-westerly, the biomass-burning
signal (fire_upwind) becomes a leading driver of ward AQI — a regional source that
local Delhi action alone cannot fully mitigate, requiring coordination with
neighbouring states.

*Summary of CAQM directions on stubble management; consult official CAQM orders
for the authoritative text.*

metadata.title: Stubble (Paddy Residue) Burning Management in NCR

citation: Stubble (Paddy Residue) Burning Management in NCR — CAQM — 2021-09-13

text:
The World Health Organization's 2021 Global Air Quality Guidelines set
recommended exposure limits below which health risks are significantly reduced.
They are health-based reference values (not legally binding standards).

metadata.title: WHO Global Air Quality Guidelines (2021)

citation: WHO Global Air Quality Guidelines (2021) — WHO — 2021-09-22

text:
- **PM2.5:** annual mean 5 µg/m³; 24-hour mean 15 µg/m³.
- **PM10:** annual mean 15 µg/m³; 24-hour mean 45 µg/m³.
- **NO2 (nitrogen dioxide):** annual mean 10 µg/m³; 24-hour mean 25 µg/m³.
- **O3 (ozone):** peak-season mean 60 µg/m³; 8-hour mean 100 µg/m³.
- **SO2 (sulphur dioxide):** 24-hour mean 40 µg/m³.
- **CO (carbon monoxide):** 24-hour mean 4 mg/m³.

metadata.title: WHO Global Air Quality Guidelines (2021)

citation: WHO Global Air Quality Guidelines (2021) — WHO — 2021-09-22

text:
Delhi's winter PM2.5 routinely exceeds the WHO 24-hour guideline (15 µg/m³) by an
order of magnitude. The guidelines also define interim targets so that cities
with high pollution can set progressive, achievable milestones on the way to the
final guideline values.

*Summary of the WHO 2021 AQG values; consult the official WHO publication for the
complete guidance and interim targets.*

metadata.title: WHO Global Air Quality Guidelines (2021)

citation: WHO Global Air Quality Guidelines (2021) — WHO — 2021-09-22
